prompt: We're 70% through our cloud migration but the business says they don't see any value. IT says things are going great. What's happening?
expected: Skill activates and names the value gap: IT is tracking proxy metrics (percent of servers migrated) that move fast, while organizational change lags, so business value has not been delivered; 70% migrated is not 70% value delivered. It should explain the S-curve and prescribe accelerating organizational change and front-loading high-value outcomes rather than defending the migration count.

prompt: Our board presentation shows we've migrated 847 of 1,200 servers to the cloud. Is this the right thing to show the board?
expected: Skill activates and identifies server count as a proxy metric that tracks effort, not value; it should apply the CTRL-F-for-'value' test, warn that IT-only metrics train the board to ask only IT questions, and recommend replacing or pairing the count with business outcome metrics (availability, deployment frequency, feature velocity, customer experience).

prompt: We had a slow start to our cloud migration but we're planning to deliver most of the value in the second half. Is this realistic?
expected: Skill activates and names this as the hockey-stick fallacy: deferred-value claims almost never materialize because the slow start reflects unresolved organizational-readiness issues that don't self-correct and sponsors lose patience before the promised upturn. It should offer the S-curve as the honest model and push to accelerate organizational change and deliver visible value now rather than banking on a later surge.

prompt: How should we sequence which applications to migrate first — easiest ones to build momentum, or something else?
expected: Skill activates and rejects sequencing by technical convenience: easy, low-value apps produce excellent proxy metrics while widening the value gap. It should prescribe ranking applications by business value contribution (availability, customer impact, feature-delivery acceleration) and migrating high-value apps first even when technically harder, building portfolio value transparency first if that ranking data is missing.

prompt: Business stakeholders are losing confidence in our cloud program. Our instinct is to accelerate the migration to show more progress. Is that the right response?
expected: Skill activates and says no: accelerating technical migration doubles down on the proxy metric when the constraint is organizational, not technical. It should recommend naming the value gap explicitly instead of defending IT metrics, accelerating organizational change, and prioritizing one or two quick high-visibility business outcomes to rebuild stakeholder confidence.

prompt: What metrics should we use to measure cloud migration success in our quarterly business review?
expected: Skill activates and provides the two-column framework: separate IT proxy metrics (servers migrated, pipelines built, automation coverage) from business value metrics (availability, deployment frequency, feature velocity, cost as percent of revenue), never presenting the former as evidence of the latter, and shifting reporting emphasis toward value metrics as the migration matures. It should also stress defining leading and lagging value metrics up front.

prompt: We need to decide whether to migrate our monolith to containers or rewrite it as microservices. What are the trade-offs?
Our enterprise cloud platform requires all developers to submit a ticket to get a new environment provisioned. Is this a problem?
We want to avoid cloud vendor lock-in. What's the best approach?
Our team writes cloud strategy principles but they all seem obvious and no one debates them. How do we make better principles?
We migrated to the cloud primarily to vacate an expiring data center contract. We met the deadline and moved 100% of workloads. Is that success?
expected: Skill recognizes this as the boundary case: for a cost-avoidance / contract-expiry migration, completion and cost are legitimate primary success metrics rather than proxy metrics, so meeting the deadline can count as success against that stated goal. But it should add that if the organization is also claiming broader business benefits, the value framing reapplies and those outcomes must be measured separately.

prompt: Our cloud program has been running for 18 months. We have great deployment automation and 90% of servers migrated. But customer satisfaction scores haven't changed. Should we be worried?
expected: Skill activates to name the value gap (strong IT metrics, flat business outcome) but surfaces the boundary nuance that customer satisfaction is a lagging indicator with a long feedback cycle; it should recommend checking leading value indicators (deployment frequency, incident rate, feature cycle time) to distinguish a genuine gap from normal metric lag, and worry if even the leading indicators are flat.
